pregunta: A data engineer wants to understand what serverless compute for Lakeflow Jobs actually manages on their behalf. Which statement is accurate?

opciones.texto_opcion: Databricks automatically manages instance types, memory, processing engine, and Databricks Runtime upgrades, with autoscaling and Photon enabled by default.
opciones.explicacion: Serverless compute continuously optimizes infrastructure and auto-upgrades the runtime, with autoscaling and Photon automatically enabled.

opciones.texto_opcion: Engineers must still configure the instance type and worker count before the job can use serverless compute.
opciones.explicacion: Serverless compute requires no cluster configuration step at all — that's its defining characteristic.

opciones.texto_opcion: Photon must be manually enabled per job when using serverless compute.
opciones.explicacion: Photon is automatically enabled on serverless compute; no manual configuration is required.

opciones.texto_opcion: Databricks Runtime version is fixed for the lifetime of the job once serverless compute is selected.
opciones.explicacion: Databricks automatically upgrades the runtime version under the hood, balancing enhancements against job stability.

pregunta: A workspace administrator is evaluating whether their workspace can use serverless compute for jobs. Which of the following is a documented requirement?

opciones.texto_opcion: The workspace must have Unity Catalog enabled and be in a supported region.
opciones.explicacion: Serverless compute for workflows requires Unity Catalog to be enabled and the workspace to be in a supported region.

opciones.texto_opcion: The workspace must disable all classic compute clusters before enabling serverless.
opciones.explicacion: Classic and serverless compute can coexist; disabling classic compute is not a requirement.

opciones.texto_opcion: Every user must individually request cluster creation permission first.
opciones.explicacion: Cluster creation permission is specifically NOT required for serverless compute — that's one of its benefits.

opciones.texto_opcion: The workspace must use credential passthrough access mode.
opciones.explicacion: Serverless compute for workflows uses standard access mode, not credential passthrough.

pregunta: A data engineer is building a job with a Spark Submit task. Why can't this task run on serverless compute?

opciones.texto_opcion: Spark Submit tasks are not supported on serverless at all; only classic jobs compute supports them.
opciones.explicacion: The compute-by-task-type reference explicitly lists Spark Submit as classic jobs only, with serverless not supported.

opciones.texto_opcion: Spark Submit tasks require Performance optimized mode to be enabled first.
opciones.explicacion: Performance optimized mode is unrelated to Spark Submit eligibility; Spark Submit simply isn't supported on serverless.

opciones.texto_opcion: Spark Submit tasks are supported on serverless only in Public Preview.
opciones.explicacion: Unlike JAR tasks (which are in Public Preview on serverless), Spark Submit has no serverless support at all.

opciones.texto_opcion: Spark Submit tasks require a serverless usage policy to be assigned.
opciones.explicacion: Usage policies control billing attribution, not task-type eligibility for serverless compute.

pregunta: A data engineer creates a new job with a notebook task and does not configure any compute settings. What compute will the task use by default?

opciones.texto_opcion: Classic all-purpose compute, since a cluster must always be explicitly selected.
opciones.explicacion: Notebook tasks default to serverless compute, not classic all-purpose, when no cluster is configured.

opciones.texto_opcion: Serverless compute, since notebook tasks select serverless by default when no cluster fields are specified.
opciones.explicacion: By default, serverless compute is selected automatically for supported task types like notebooks, requiring no cluster configuration step.

opciones.texto_opcion: The job will fail to create until a cluster is specified.
opciones.explicacion: Job creation does not fail — serverless is auto-selected for supported task types.

opciones.texto_opcion: A shared job cluster scoped to the entire job run.
opciones.explicacion: Shared job clusters are a classic-compute concept; the default for a notebook task with no cluster fields is serverless.

pregunta: A data engineer reviews two performance mode settings for a job's serverless tasks. What is the key trade-off between Standard performance and Performance optimized mode?

opciones.texto_opcion: They use different SKUs entirely, with Performance optimized being a premium hardware tier.
opciones.explicacion: Both modes use the same SKU; they differ in DBU consumption and startup latency, not hardware tier.

opciones.texto_opcion: Standard performance has lower DBU consumption with ~4–6 minute startup latency; Performance optimized starts and runs faster but consumes more DBUs.
opciones.explicacion: This matches the documented trade-off exactly: Standard saves cost with slower startup, Performance optimized trades higher DBU usage for speed.

opciones.texto_opcion: Standard performance is only available for SQL tasks, while Performance optimized is only available for notebook tasks.
opciones.explicacion: Both modes apply to a job's serverless tasks generally, not restricted by task type in this way.

opciones.texto_opcion: Performance optimized mode disables Photon to reduce startup time.
opciones.explicacion: Photon remains enabled on serverless compute; Performance optimized mode is about DBU consumption and startup speed, not disabling Photon.

pregunta: A data engineer wants to configure Performance optimized mode for a one-time job run submitted directly via the `runs/submit` REST API endpoint, expecting Standard performance to be available as a fallback. What does the documentation say about this scenario?

opciones.texto_opcion: Standard performance mode is not supported for one-time runs created via `runs/submit`.
opciones.explicacion: This is explicitly documented: Standard performance mode is not supported for one-time runs created through the `runs/submit` endpoint.

opciones.texto_opcion: Performance optimized mode is not supported for one-time runs created via `runs/submit`.
opciones.explicacion: It's Standard performance that's unsupported for `runs/submit`, not Performance optimized.

opciones.texto_opcion: Both modes are fully supported for `runs/submit` with no restrictions.
opciones.explicacion: There is a documented restriction — Standard performance mode is not supported for `runs/submit` one-time runs.

opciones.texto_opcion: `runs/submit` does not support serverless compute at all.
opciones.explicacion: Serverless compute is supported via `runs/submit`; the restriction is specifically about Standard performance mode.

pregunta: An organization assigns a new serverless usage policy to a team for billing attribution purposes. The team has several existing jobs that were created before the policy assignment. What happens to those existing jobs?

opciones.texto_opcion: They are automatically tagged with the new usage policy the next time they run.
opciones.explicacion: Existing jobs are explicitly NOT automatically tagged once a user is assigned a policy.

opciones.texto_opcion: They must be manually updated to attach the new usage policy.
opciones.explicacion: The documentation states existing jobs are not automatically tagged — they must be manually updated to attach a policy.

opciones.texto_opcion: They will fail to run until a usage policy is attached.
opciones.explicacion: Jobs without a manually attached policy continue to run; they simply aren't retroactively tagged for the new policy.

opciones.texto_opcion: They are automatically deleted and must be recreated under the new policy.
opciones.explicacion: No deletion occurs; manual update is required to attach a policy, not recreation.

pregunta: A data engineer tries to set `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions` at the compute level for a serverless job, the same way they would for a classic cluster. What is the correct behavior on serverless compute?

opciones.texto_opcion: Compute-scoped configuration isn't supported on serverless; allowed Spark parameters can only be set at the session level, e.g., inside the notebook.
opciones.explicacion: Serverless compute restricts Spark configuration to a specific allow-list and only permits setting it at the session level, not the compute level.

opciones.texto_opcion: Any Spark configuration parameter can be set at the compute level on serverless, identical to classic clusters.
opciones.explicacion: Serverless restricts both which parameters can be set and where — compute-level configuration is not supported.

opciones.texto_opcion: Spark configuration parameters must be set via compute policies on serverless.
opciones.explicacion: Compute policies are explicitly listed as not supported on serverless compute.

opciones.texto_opcion: Spark configuration parameters can only be set through environment variables on serverless.
opciones.explicacion: Environment variables are explicitly listed as not supported on serverless; session-level notebook configuration is the supported mechanism.

pregunta: A data engineer needs to add a new Python library dependency to a notebook task running on serverless compute. What is the supported approach?

opciones.texto_opcion: Install the library as a cluster-level library in the compute configuration.
opciones.explicacion: Cluster-level libraries are a classic-compute concept; serverless compute manages dependencies through the serverless environment, not cluster-level libraries.

opciones.texto_opcion: Use notebook-scoped libraries, managed through the serverless environment settings.
opciones.explicacion: On serverless, dependency configuration is managed through serverless environment settings, with notebook-scoped libraries as the supported mechanism.

opciones.texto_opcion: Add the library as a JAR library directly inside the notebook.
opciones.explicacion: JAR libraries are explicitly not supported inside notebooks on serverless compute.

opciones.texto_opcion: Use a compute-scoped init script to install the library at cluster startup.
opciones.explicacion: Compute-scoped init scripts are explicitly not supported on serverless compute.

pregunta: A notebook task on serverless compute is running out of memory while processing a large dataset. According to the documented Public Preview feature, what option is available?

opciones.texto_opcion: Configure a High Memory setting in the notebook's Environment side panel — available only for notebook task types.
opciones.explicacion: High Memory for notebook tasks is a Public Preview feature configured via the Environment side panel and is only available for notebook task types.

opciones.texto_opcion: Switch the task to a memory-optimized instance type in the cluster configuration.
opciones.explicacion: Serverless compute doesn't expose instance type selection; instead, a documented High Memory toggle exists for notebook tasks specifically.

opciones.texto_opcion: Enable High Memory via a Spark configuration parameter at the session level.
opciones.explicacion: High Memory is configured through the Environment side panel UI setting, not a Spark session-level configuration parameter.

opciones.texto_opcion: High memory is available for all task types, not just notebooks, once enabled.
opciones.explicacion: The feature is explicitly scoped to notebook task types only.

pregunta: A job runs a non-idempotent task where re-running it could cause duplicate side effects (e.g., sending a one-time external notification). The engineer wants the task to execute at most once. What should they do?

opciones.texto_opcion: Leave auto-optimization enabled, since Databricks recommends it for all workloads regardless of idempotency.
opciones.explicacion: Auto-optimization is recommended for idempotent workloads; for non-idempotent, at-most-once tasks it should be explicitly disabled.

opciones.texto_opcion: In the task's Retry Policy dialog, uncheck 'Enable serverless auto-optimization (may include additional retries)'.
opciones.explicacion: This matches the documented steps for disabling auto-optimization per task for non-idempotent, at-most-once workloads.

opciones.texto_opcion: Switch the task to classic compute, since serverless always retries regardless of configuration.
opciones.explicacion: Auto-optimization/retries can be explicitly disabled per task on serverless itself; switching compute types is unnecessary.

opciones.texto_opcion: Set `spark.databricks.execution.timeout` to 0 to disable retries.
opciones.explicacion: That Spark property controls query execution timeout, not the auto-optimization/retry behavior.

pregunta: A data engineer wants to share a single compute resource across multiple tasks in a classic job to reduce cluster start-up latency. Which risk is specifically associated with this shared-compute pattern?

opciones.texto_opcion: Tasks sharing the same driver JVM can be affected by persisting Scala singleton/companion object state across tasks.
opciones.explicacion: When tasks share a jobs compute resource, they run on the same driver JVM, so Scala singleton/companion object state can persist and be overwritten by concurrent tasks.

opciones.texto_opcion: Shared job clusters can be reused by other jobs after the current job run completes.
opciones.explicacion: A shared job cluster is scoped to a single job run and cannot be reused by other jobs or other runs of the same job.

opciones.texto_opcion: Libraries must be declared at the shared cluster level rather than per task.
opciones.explicacion: It's the opposite — libraries cannot be declared at the shared job cluster level and must be declared in each task's settings.

opciones.texto_opcion: This sharing behavior also applies to serverless compute and must be manually configured there too.
opciones.explicacion: Serverless compute is provisioned per task automatically and does not require manual cluster-sharing configuration like classic compute.

pregunta: A streaming job currently uses the implicit default trigger (no trigger specified) inside a notebook task on serverless compute. What will happen when this job runs?

opciones.texto_opcion: It runs successfully using `Trigger.ProcessingTime("0 seconds")` as the implicit default, just like on classic compute.
opciones.explicacion: Serverless does not support the implicit default ProcessingTime trigger — an explicit supported trigger must be set.

opciones.texto_opcion: It raises `INFINITE_STREAMING_TRIGGER_NOT_SUPPORTED`, since serverless does not support default or time-based interval triggers.
opciones.explicacion: Serverless explicitly does not support default/time-based interval triggers, including the implicit `Trigger.ProcessingTime("0 seconds")` default, and raises this error.

opciones.texto_opcion: It automatically falls back to `Trigger.AvailableNow()` without any error.
opciones.explicacion: There is no automatic fallback — an unsupported trigger raises an explicit error; the engineer must set a supported trigger explicitly.

opciones.texto_opcion: It runs successfully but switches the job to classic compute automatically.
opciones.explicacion: There is no automatic compute-switching behavior; the unsupported trigger simply raises an error.

pregunta: A team needs a Structured Streaming workload to run on serverless compute. Which trigger is recommended?

opciones.texto_opcion: `Trigger.ProcessingTime("10 seconds")`
opciones.explicacion: Time-based interval triggers like `Trigger.ProcessingTime` are not supported on serverless compute.

opciones.texto_opcion: `Trigger.Continuous("1 second")`
opciones.explicacion: `Trigger.Continuous` is explicitly not supported on serverless compute.

opciones.texto_opcion: `Trigger.AvailableNow()`
opciones.explicacion: `Trigger.AvailableNow()` is the recommended, fully supported trigger for batch-style streaming ingestion on serverless compute.

opciones.texto_opcion: `Trigger.Once()`
opciones.explicacion: `Trigger.Once()` is supported on serverless but deprecated and not recommended; `Trigger.AvailableNow()` is the recommended choice.

pregunta: A data engineer needs a workload to run essentially continuously on serverless compute, but a job-level Continuous trigger is not supported there. What is the documented alternative?

opciones.texto_opcion: Use a continuous-mode Lakeflow Spark Declarative Pipeline, which is serverless-compatible, instead of a job-level Continuous trigger.
opciones.explicacion: For continuous streaming-style workloads on serverless, the documented approach is continuous pipeline mode in a Lakeflow Spark Declarative Pipeline.

opciones.texto_opcion: Set `Trigger.ProcessingTime("0 seconds")` explicitly to simulate continuous behavior.
opciones.explicacion: This is exactly the unsupported implicit default trigger on serverless and raises an error rather than simulating continuous behavior.

opciones.texto_opcion: Enable Performance optimized mode, which unlocks Continuous trigger support on serverless.
opciones.explicacion: Performance optimized mode affects DBU consumption and startup latency, not trigger type support; Continuous triggers remain unsupported regardless.

opciones.texto_opcion: There is no alternative; continuous workloads cannot run on serverless compute in any form.
opciones.explicacion: A documented alternative exists — continuous pipeline mode in a Lakeflow Spark Declarative Pipeline — so this is not entirely true.

opciones.texto_opcion: Serverless compute, since omitting cluster-related fields on a supported task type results in serverless by default.
opciones.explicacion: Omitting `new_cluster` and `existing_cluster_id` for a supported task type like a notebook results in the task running on serverless compute by default.

opciones.texto_opcion: The API call will fail validation due to a missing cluster field.
opciones.explicacion: This is valid syntax — omitting cluster fields is the documented way to default to serverless, not an error.

opciones.texto_opcion: Classic all-purpose compute, since the API defaults to classic when fields are omitted.
opciones.explicacion: The documented default behavior when cluster fields are omitted is serverless, not classic all-purpose.

opciones.texto_opcion: The task will run on whichever compute resource was used by the previous run of the same job.
opciones.explicacion: There is no such fallback behavior; omission of cluster fields explicitly defaults to serverless for supported task types.

pregunta:
A data engineer reviews the following Databricks Asset Bundle definition:

```yaml
resources:
  jobs:
    mixed_compute_job:
      name: mixed_compute_job
      tasks:
        - task_key: ingest
          notebook_task:
            notebook_path: ../src/ingest.ipynb

        - task_key: legacy_jar_step
          depends_on:
            - task_key: ingest
          new_cluster:
            spark_version: 15.4.x-scala2.12
            node_type_id: i3.xlarge
            num_workers: 2
          libraries:
            - jar: ../dist/legacy_step.jar
          spark_jar_task:
            main_class_name: com.example.LegacyStep
```

Which statement correctly describes the compute used by this job?

opciones.texto_opcion: Both tasks run on serverless compute since the job has no top-level cluster definition.
opciones.explicacion: The `legacy_jar_step` task explicitly defines a `new_cluster` block, so it runs on classic compute, not serverless.

opciones.texto_opcion: The `ingest` task runs on serverless by default since it has no cluster fields; `legacy_jar_step` runs on classic compute via its explicit `new_cluster` definition.
opciones.explicacion: This correctly mixes compute types within one job — `ingest` defaults to serverless, while `legacy_jar_step` explicitly uses a classic `new_cluster`.

opciones.texto_opcion: This configuration is invalid — a single job cannot mix serverless and classic compute across tasks.
opciones.explicacion: Mixing compute types across tasks in the same job is explicitly supported and may be required when a task type isn't supported by serverless.

opciones.texto_opcion: Both tasks run on classic compute because the `new_cluster` block on one task applies to the entire job.
opciones.explicacion: Cluster definitions are scoped per task, not job-wide; `ingest` still defaults to serverless independently.

pregunta: A finance team wants to monitor and attribute the cost of jobs running on serverless compute across different projects. Where should they look?

opciones.texto_opcion: The billable usage system table (`system.access.billing`), which includes user and workload attributes for serverless costs.
opciones.explicacion: Serverless job costs are monitored via the billable usage system table, which includes user and workload attributes for granular cost tracking.

opciones.texto_opcion: The Spark UI's Executors tab, which aggregates DBU spend per job.
opciones.explicacion: The Spark UI Executors tab shows runtime executor metrics, not billing or DBU cost attribution; that comes from the billable usage system table.

opciones.texto_opcion: The job's Retry Policy dialog, which displays historical cost per retry.
opciones.explicacion: The Retry Policy dialog controls auto-optimization/retry behavior, not cost reporting.

opciones.texto_opcion: The Lakeflow Jobs UI Timeline view, which shows DBU cost per task.
opciones.explicacion: The Timeline view shows task execution and query details, not DBU billing costs; cost monitoring is done via the billable usage system table.

pregunta: On an invoice, how does serverless compute for jobs running on Azure appear, compared to AWS or GCP?

opciones.texto_opcion: It appears as 'Jobs Serverless Compute' on all clouds including Azure.
opciones.explicacion: 'Jobs Serverless Compute' is the label used on AWS/GCP; Azure uses a different label.

opciones.texto_opcion: It appears as 'Automated Serverless Compute' on Azure, while AWS/GCP show 'Jobs Serverless Compute'.
opciones.explicacion: The documentation specifies distinct invoice labels: 'Jobs Serverless Compute' for AWS/GCP and 'Automated Serverless Compute' for Azure.

opciones.texto_opcion: It appears as 'Jobs Compute (Photon)' on Azure since Photon is always enabled.
opciones.explicacion: 'Jobs Compute (Photon)' is the label for classic compute with Photon, not serverless compute on any cloud.

opciones.texto_opcion: Azure does not bill separately for serverless compute; it's bundled into general workspace charges.
opciones.explicacion: Azure does have a distinct invoice label for serverless compute — 'Automated Serverless Compute' — it is not bundled.

pregunta: A team is preparing to migrate a classic compute job to serverless. According to documented best practices, which of the following should they audit for first as a likely migration blocker?

opciones.texto_opcion: Whether the job uses Performance optimized mode, since it cannot be enabled during migration.
opciones.explicacion: Performance optimized mode is a serverless-only setting that's simply configured after migration, not a migration blocker to audit for.

opciones.texto_opcion: R or Scala UDF usage, DBFS mounts with instance profiles, Hive SerDe tables, dataframe caching APIs, and compute-scoped libraries.
opciones.explicacion: These are explicitly called out as the most common blockers surfaced by the serverless compute limitations list before migrating a classic job to serverless.

opciones.texto_opcion: Whether the job currently uses a serverless usage policy.
opciones.explicacion: Usage policies are a billing attribution feature that's configured for serverless jobs, not a pre-migration compatibility blocker for jobs still on classic compute.

opciones.texto_opcion: Whether the job's notebook tasks use High Memory mode.
opciones.explicacion: High Memory is a serverless-only feature that becomes available after migration; it isn't a documented compatibility blocker to check beforehand.
